summary: Typosquat of Codebuff CLI fetching opaque binary from unrelated GitHub account at mutable tag
details: Package `codebuff-cli` ships a README copied verbatim from the legitimate `codebuff` package by CodebuffAI (instructing users to `npm install -g codebuff` and linking to github.com/CodebuffAI/codebuff), but is published under a different name and the bin wrapper at cli/bin/codebuff.cjs:10 hardcodes `REPO = 'Marcus-Mok-GH/codebuff-cli'` — an unrelated personal GitHub account. On first CLI invocation the wrapper queries `releases/latest` of that personal repo, downloads `codebuff-<platform>-<arch>` to `~/.codebuff/bin/`, chmods it 0755, and execs it with the user's argv (cli/bin/codebuff.cjs lines 198, 207, 213). The URL is a mutable `latest` pointer (not pinned to the package version), the publisher does not match the impersonated project, no hash or signature is verified, and the executed binary is not shipped in the tarball — the author can swap the bytes at any time and every existing install picks them up on the next run. The README also documents the default backend as `https://fireworks-api-backend-clviguvqt-sjdjdiejdrirhdkjejs-projects.vercel.app/` — a random Vercel preview deployment with a gibberish team slug, not codebuff.com — to which the dropped binary directs requests, so users believing they are using Codebuff will send their prompts and any configured credentials to an unrelated host. Joint signals (typosquat + impersonation README + mutable-tag opaque binary from non-publisher account + no integrity check + attacker-controlled default backend) match the generic-binary-runner-dropper pattern.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
